mnemonics.title: TCP Congestion Control Phases
mnemonics.content: S-C-F-A: Start with Slow Start, move to Congestion Avoidance when ssthresh reached, use Fast Retransmit on 3 dupACKs, Fast Recovery to recover

mnemonics.title: RED Thresholds
mnemonics.content: RED Drop Logic: Below min - keep all | Between min & max - drop probabilistically | Above max - drop all

acronyms.usage: Core TCP congestion control principle where cwnd increases linearly and decreases multiplicatively during congestion
The maximum amount of data the sender can transmit without receiving ACKs
Threshold value that determines when TCP transitions from Slow Start to Congestion Avoidance
Time taken for a packet to travel to destination and back; used to calculate network delay
The largest amount of data that can be sent in a single TCP segment; typically 1460 bytes
Active Queue Management algorithm that drops packets probabilistically before congestion occurs
Techniques to guarantee specific performance levels for different traffic types
Queue management techniques like RED that proactively manage congestion

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a staircase: Slow Start = steep exponential stairs (doubling each step), Congestion Avoidance = shallow linear stairs (one step at a time)
Think of a car's accelerator (additive increase = gradual speed up) and brake (multiplicative decrease = sudden brake to half speed)
Like water leaking from a bucket at constant rate - regardless of how fast you pour water in, it drips steadily
Like a museum ticket system - you need tokens to enter, tokens accumulate up to a limit, but once bucket is empty, you must wait for more tokens

analogies.analogy: Network congestion is like highway traffic jam - too many cars (packets) trying to use limited roads (bandwidth), causing slowdowns and accidents (packet loss)
Flow control is like a restaurant waiter checking if the customer can handle more food, while congestion control is like the kitchen checking if they can handle more orders overall
